speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] before World War II in Germany. What year was that when Hitler was elected into power?

speaker: interviewer
text: You said your parents voted.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When the results came out, what was-- what was the atmosphere at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we were shocked, but for at least-- for the first day or two, we were not aware of the extent to which this was going to change our lives. It took a little while to-- to find out just how serious this-- these new holders of the-- of the political power would be in the application of their crazy ideas. The-- the anticipation was that, just like everybody else, once they had the responsibility of power they would act fairly normally. But we were wrong.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were how old at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was 17 and 1/2.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you understood.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you understand, and what did you understand?

speaker: interviewee
text: I understood that here was a radical, right, anti-Semitic party which had gotten a bare majority in-- being in cahoots with the next most national-minded party, the [SPEAKING GERMAN]. And in as much as they-- their companions were really serious and well-behaved citizens, we assumed that they would teach manners to these ruffians who had-- who had grabbed the-- the top position. But it didn't work out that way.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were telling us that Hitler used to come to your town.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you ever seen him?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What school did--

speaker: interviewee
text: That is, not-- not in person.

speaker: interviewer
text: Person. What school did you go to when-- when this happened, when the change happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: When the change happened, I was in the [NON-ENGLISH], which was the [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] 11th grade of the-- of the school, the-- the high school to which I went. And was, incidentally, the-- the top grade of the school in Eberbach. And then for the remaining two years I had to commute to Heidelberg in order to complete the 13 year course of the school. And the most visible change after-- I don't remember whether it was after a week or so, that the flag of the country was changed. The black, red, and gold flag of the-- of the Wiemar Republic was eliminated, and the Swastika flag, black, white, and red, was hoisted in the-- in the school. The director of the school, who was a registered Democrat, walked out and said, I refuse to teach under this flag. And he was immediately arrested. He was very lucky in that they just reduced his rank from director to teacher. But we realized that [LAUGHS] a different wind was blowing.

speaker: interviewer
text: The attitude of the teachers, did that change?

speaker: interviewee
text: I beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: The attitude of the teachers.

speaker: interviewee
text: The attitude of-- of teachers did change, yes. And those who, prior to the arrival of the Nazi government, may have been quiet members of the Nazi party, they became the big shots. And the first thing that I-- that I learned at the end of the school year-- which in Germany, or at least in our state, was around April-- the-- the custom in-- in the school was that the first student, the top student in each class, got a prize. Every student got-- every first student got a prize, except I. I kept my first position, because that was the statistics, but the-- the teacher in charge of the class was a Nazi, and he said, in my class, no Jew will get a prize, period.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you feel any change amongst the students?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, but it was not in any violent or crass way, but a slow process of not just not being part of the-- of the group, of not being invited to parties, and all that type of thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: But in the class, were there any changes in the class?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the-- as long as I was in high school, both in Eberbach and later in Heidelberg, scholastically, there was no major change.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you commute to Heidelberg?

speaker: interviewee
text: By train.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any danger for a Jew being on that train?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not-- not really, although I was conscious that under certain circumstances, there might be. I-- as far as I recall, I-- I never was especially self-conscious on the-- on the train. But since I was the only Jew of-- of my age in the Eberbach, I would go to a neighboring town, even smaller than-- than Eberbach, to meet, where there were about three or four Jews of roughly my age.

speaker: interviewer
text: So on my commuter's ticket, I could-- I could take the train to that town, which was about halfway between Eberbach and Heidelberg, and whenever I went there, I took my-- my camera. Why? Because that gave me reason to also carry a heavy tripod that folded up to about this length and had a very heavy metal head, so that if I would be in any way attacked, at least I would have a chance at defending myself. I also knew that that would have relatively little meaning, because the Nazis would never attack one on one. Would always be a whole group against one. Your train pass. Did-- did the Jews have different train passes than non-Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, certainly not at that time. And whether they did in the-- in-- in later times, I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any identification cards?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, the-- the train-- the-- the commuter ticket itself was-- had a picture identification, but-- but I do not remember that that had any mark for identifying me as a Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you decide, or did you decide, to leave the country?

speaker: interviewee
text: The decision to leave the country came with the opportunity to leave the country. In that I think I am unique. I am one of only five people who had the same experience. After graduating from high school and passing the Abitur examination, I started studying at the [NON-ENGLISH], which was the liberal rabbinical seminary in Berlin. Very soon after arriving there, I found out that a-- a letter had arrived from the Hebrew Union College in Cincinnati asking the seminary to send five of its students to Cincinnati to complete their studies there. The-- Dr. Julian Morgenstern, the president of the Hebrew Union College, had a feeling for what was going to happen in Germany, even though only the beginnings of the persecutions and the exclusion of Jews had happened. And he was farsighted enough to feel that this would make a difference. And indeed it made a difference between life and death for-- I'm sure, for some of us. So I applied for one of those five positions, and strangely, out of a student body of probably 150 or so, there were only about eight or nine who applied, of whom six were found to be eligible as far as the administration of the school was concerned. So one day, the two youngest of those six were asked to come to the dean's office, and we were told that the remaining four applicants were definitely accepted, but since there were only five spaces, only one of the two of us, being the two youngest and the most recent arrivals at the seminary, would be able to go. Just as the dean prepared a couple of pieces of paper so that we could draw and see who will draw the long and who would draw the short, the door opened, and one of the other four came in and said that his parents would not permit him to leave, which meant that both of us could go. So that was in April-- no, in-- in May of 1935. And in September, the five of us started for the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's go back. When you applied, did your parents know that you are applying to leave the country?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. It was made very clear that we could go only with our family's approval.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the situation at home at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: The business was still functioning. People who shopped there knew that they were exposing themselves to insult from the-- from the Nazis. Many who-- certainly nobody who worked for that [NON-ENGLISH], the major industry, would dare to buy in the Jewish store. But there were still people who had the guts to buy from Jews. But that number decreased constantly, and the threats against them were constant.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did-- when did the average life of Jews begin to change in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, what I've mentioned a moment ago certainly changed the lifestyle of most Jews who were still employed. The immediate changes, that came within-- in some cases, within days, sometimes within weeks or months of the Nazis taking over, is that anybody in public employ was fired. The-- anybody who was-- who had public visibility, actors for instance, and theaters, most of which were publicly supported theaters, they were fired The-- in-- in some of the occupations, they lost their jobs. So the-- the lives of those people changed almost immediately. The reaction of the Jewish community was dramatic, in that within a-- a few months, there were Jewish organizations that just hadn't existed before. Welfare organizations, because they-- you had to take care of people who had lost their income. Cultural organizations, the-- for instance, in-- in Heidelberg, which had a Jewish community of maybe 200 or 300 families, we started a little theater group. I played the-- the lead in a performance of 'Jacob's Dream' by Beer-Hofmann, a play which had been performed in Germany, but, of course, could not be performed anymore, because Beer-Hofmann was Jewish. The Jewish orchestras were formed, et cetera, et cetera. The-- the Jewish response to being isolated from the rest of the community was a very constructive and creative response. The-- and for a while, it was very effective. But then the emigration became-- developed from a trickle into-- into a stream, and the most productive people of the community began to disappear.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were these changes government regulated, or were they individually applied?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in Germany, almost anything, even in-- in a democratic Weimar Republic, the government had a great deal to say about-- about any organized activity. But from the moment the Nazis came to power, everything had to be approved by the government. So all these things were approved by the Nazis, but what created them was the spirit of the community.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did your parents lose their business?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am not entirely certain. The-- as far as I recall, when I visited there in 1937, the-- I was in the United States on a student visa from 1935 to 1937, then had to go back in order to change that student visa into an immigration visa that gave me the chance to settle here permanently. For that purpose I went back to Germany in the summer of 1937 during my school vacation, and I spent, I think, about six or seven weeks in-- in Eberbach.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll get to that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's go back before you left. Before you left for the United States--

speaker: interviewee
text: Before I left for the United States, my-- the-- at least officially, my German-- my parents still owned and conducted their business.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your grandparents were still involved?

speaker: interviewee
text: Still were partners, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe how was it when you left your home. How did you prepare to leave your home, and-- and what was the atmosphere, and what was the interaction with your parents and grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] The-- the feeling of the family was that I had definitely made the right decision, because Germany was no place for a Jew to grow up. And certainly there was very little educational and professional chance, the-- even though the one course that I had chosen [LAUGHS] still was there as long as there would be a sizable Jewish community. So they agreed fully with my intent to emigrate. And the only question was where to get the necessary funds, because my parents' income had definitely been affected by the Nazi boycott of the store. So the B'nai B'rith, the state organization of the B'nai B'rith in the state of Baden gave me a-- a grant to purchase the tickets. But all this was something that was done and had become very similar to what was happening in Jewish families all over who did want to utilize the first chance they had to get out of the country.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your parents think of leaving at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, as long as they had an income, they felt that this was where they would hold out until what they considered the inevitable end of this Nazi nonsense. That that end would not come until another World War was something that was only vaguely feared, but did not look like a reality.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had a passport?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your passport identify you as a Jew?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Not yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- did you go to the train station? Did your parents take you to the train station?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not only that, they-- they traveled with me as far as Mannheim, which was the next largest city, where I caught the train for-- for Paris, where-- and I met the other four-- three of the other four students in Paris. The fifth one was not to join us until Southampton, where the-- where the ship stopped.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have transit visas through these countries?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, of course. We-- oh, through France I don't think we needed a transit visa, but if we did, we had it, yes. That was routine.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you come to the United States? What transportation?

speaker: interviewee
text: By ship from Le Havre, France, with a stop in Southampton to New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which ship?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was the Britannic, a Cunard liner.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did the journey last?

speaker: interviewee
text: 10 days. We left on September 5 from Le Havre, stopped in Southampton on the next day, and then arrived in New York on the 15th of September.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was almost 20.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember of the journey?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] What I remember most is that of those nine or 10 days that we spent on the ship, four or five were a terrible, terrible storm, which I spent flat on my-- on my back, except for the times when I had to bend over the-- the side of the bed and get rid of what little I had eaten.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you, amongst yourselves, the four students-- there were four of you? Three of you?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were five of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Five of you. Did you have any conversations about what the future is going to be like, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, of course. That was the-- the big-- the big riddle of the future, the-- I'm-- don't know about the others, but I had never heard of the Hebrew Union College until I learned about this opportunity. And, of course, we felt that Americans probably knew very little about Judaism. We learned that several of the-- of the teachers at the Hebrew Union College had done their graduate work in Berlin.

speaker: interviewer
text: So since they had studied at the same place we did, they must know no more than we did as students. Of course, we were wrong. But there was the tendency of thinking that oh, of course, we in Europe have so much more knowledge than these people in that new country over in America. Did you talk about what you left behind?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course. The-- we still felt very much part of-- of those families, if-- if that's what you mean by what we left behind. Obviously our major, if not sole, contact with Germany from that moment on were our families. And what happened to them was very much of a concern to-- to us. The-- if I'm not going too far ahead, the-- when we had news of Kristallnacht--

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's hold that for a minute. I-- I was wondering whether you had any fear of your family while you were on-- on this journey. Were you afraid when are you going to see them again, what their fate is going to be, were you thinking of those-- on those terms?

speaker: interviewee
text: Vaguely. Vaguely, perhaps. We're-- if we thought theoretically, yes. But by that time our-- we had not been affected in any essential way by the Nazis. The life for Jews had become far more uncomfortable. And we knew that there-- there was a-- a-- some-- some danger, but we-- we were not as conscious of the radical nature of the danger as apparently were some more farsighted Americans who were wise enough to want to-- to start take people out of Germany and give them a chance for survival. No. Yes, we-- we-- we were very much concerned about our families, but were not aware of the real danger.

speaker: interviewer
text: As you were nearing the-- the-- the United States, describe that last moment of-- before arriving.

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- there was-- the best description for that time between our leaving and our arriving was one big question mark. We did not know what we were in for. We had, of course, an idea of-- of what we would have to study, because we had started to study for the rabbinate. But we did-- had no awareness of-- of what school life was like in America, how it would differ from-- from Germany, what we could take for granted and what would definitely be a-- a new experience. The-- it-- it was an exciting thing, because obviously we knew that we were going to be among friends, because they were thoughtful enough of us to-- to bring us there. But what that would mean, we did not know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you speak the language?

speaker: interviewee
text: The--

speaker: interviewer
text: English.

speaker: interviewee
text: --two-- two of us were fairly fluent in-- in English. The remaining three had studied English, but did not speak it very well. But they learned fast once they get to this country-- got to this country.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were coming to the shores, what was the first thing you saw?

speaker: interviewee
text: The skyline of New York, something which of course we had seen in pictures many times. But to see that skyline and to see the Statue of Liberty, that was just the thrill of our lives.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to change that tape again, so we'll stop for a minute.
